subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree, completed at age 20
Undergraduate major was likely in Mechanical Engineering, Computer Science, or a related STEM field
Received foundational training in numerical methods and algorithm design during university studies
Completed coursework involving programming in languages such as C++
Participated in a senior project or research experience focusing on computational modeling

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Computer Numerically Controlled (CNC) Tool Operator
Role involves developing and implementing new numerical algorithms for physical modeling
Responsible for validating and proving properties of numerical algorithms
Engages in reading and extending academic research papers related to numerical computation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works within the manufacturing/advanced engineering sector
Role is at the intersection of applied mathematics, software development, and physical modeling
Operates in an environment where computational research and practical implementation overlap

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Likely employed by a university research group, R&D department, or small to mid-sized tech-oriented company
Works in a team-based environment with mentorship from a supervising professor or senior engineer
Collaborates primarily with a small group of researchers or engineers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business focus is on computational modeling, simulation, and numerical algorithm development
Operates in a niche segment dealing with scientific computing for engineering applications
Primary clients or stakeholders are academic groups, engineering departments, or manufacturing firms needing advanced computation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has been in the current role for less than 1 year
Started position shortly after completing undergraduate studies
Early-career professional, still building foundational expertise in applied computational work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level position, typical for recent graduates
Works under supervision but is developing autonomy on research and implementation tasks
Not yet in a managerial or lead role, but beginning to take initiative in technical problem-solving

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Reads recent academic papers to stay updated on advances in numerical computation
Identifies potential extensions or improvements to existing numerical algorithms
Designs and implements new algorithms in C++ and Julia
Documents and tests code components to verify correctness and efficiency
Collaborates with a supervising professor for guidance and feedback

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of time reading and analyzing research papers
Roughly 35% of time is dedicated to planning and designing new algorithms
Around 25% is spent coding, testing, and integrating software components

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has moderate autonomy in selecting which research extensions to pursue
Makes independent decisions about algorithm design and coding approaches
Receives direction on high-level research topics from supervising professor
Consults with peers or supervisor for critical decisions or novel problems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Skimming abstracts and conclusions to quickly assess paper relevance
Writing code for algorithmic components after thorough planning
Using Archive and paper search tools to locate relevant literature

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Proving mathematical properties or correctness of novel algorithms
Integrating multiple complex code modules into a cohesive solution
Extending research into unexplored or highly technical areas

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Reading and summarizing multiple academic papers with similar content
Unit testing code for standard edge cases across different implementations
Formatting and documenting code for consistency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys creative aspects such as designing new algorithms or solving complex numerical problems
Finds deep dives into novel research topics particularly engaging
Considers routine code maintenance and documentation more tedious
Repetitive testing and paperwork are least preferred

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Difficulty in quickly extracting key insights from dense academic papers
Time-consuming manual coding for functions with clear specifications
Occasional lack of clarity in research paper methods requiring repeated readings
Manual integration and debugging of code modules can be cumbersome

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Generally most productive when working on algorithm design, which is also most enjoyable
Skill level is highest in coding and literature review, supporting higher productivity in these areas
Tedious or repetitive tasks can lower both enjoyment and motivation, slightly reducing productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and curious about adopting new programming tools or libraries
Sees technological learning as essential for staying effective in the role
Positively motivated by opportunities to improve workflow with tech

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new programming environments such as Julia notebooks
Comfortable exploring new code editors and research databases
Willing to experiment with new tools if they offer clear workflow benefits

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate personal time to learning high-impact technologies
Prefers hands-on experimentation over formal training sessions
May prioritize tech training if it is directly relevant to current projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by direct relevance of new technology to current research or coding tasks
Workload and time constraints can limit ability to explore unrelated tools
Preference for tech with strong community support and good documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Supervising professor’s recommendations strongly influence tool adoption
Peers’ positive experiences with new tools encourage exploration
Open discussions with colleagues can accelerate willingness to try emerging technologies

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Regularly uses Archive (arXiv) for discovering and downloading research papers
Codes primarily in C++ and Julia for algorithm implementation
Utilizes Visual Studio Code for editing and debugging C++ code
Employs Pluto and Quarto notebooks for interactive Julia development

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on version control systems such as Git for code management
Uses LaTeX or Markdown for documenting research and code
Employs paper-based note-taking and planning for algorithm design

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with best practices in C++ and Julia coding standards
Experienced with interactive coding environments like Pluto notebooks
Aware of standard research tools for literature review and citation management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces compatibility issues when sharing code between C++ and Julia environments
Has to manually transfer results or data between different software platforms
Not all interactive notebooks integrate seamlessly with version control or documentation tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Has a basic to intermediate understanding of AI/ML concepts from self-study and academic exposure
Recognizes core terms such as 'language model', 'automation', and 'summarization'
Has used AI tools primarily for text-based summarization and code generation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Has used large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT occasionally for research tasks
May have experimented with Copilot for code suggestions within Visual Studio Code
Has not yet adopted specialized AI platforms for scientific computing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI tools occasionally, mainly for paper summarization or extracting key points
Sometimes applies AI assistants for generating code snippets or boilerplate functions
Explores AI-driven literature search to speed up paper discovery

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used LLMs to quickly summarize dense academic abstracts, saving reading time
Experienced inaccurate or overly generic code suggestions from AI, requiring careful review
Learned that AI-generated content must always be double-checked for technical accuracy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal AI training provided by the organization to date
Relies on online tutorials and peer advice for learning AI tool usage
Occasionally shares tips and findings about AI tools with colleagues

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers to tackle problem-solving independently, using AI tools as on-demand assistants
Values step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex or unfamiliar coding tasks
Switches between independent work and AI collaboration depending on task complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as a smart assistant for summarization and code generation
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor for brainstorming algorithm extensions
Does not rely on AI as a full co-worker, preferring to retain human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating more AI-driven features if they enhance productivity
Willing to redesign workflows to incorporate AI for repetitive or data-intensive tasks
Prefers gradual adoption of AI tools, with time to evaluate their reliability

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational interfaces intuitive for summarization or brainstorming tasks
Prefers command-based interfaces when generating code or running specific functions
Switches interface preference based on the nature of the task and needed precision

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Algorithm design and proof tasks require deep domain expertise not easily replaced by AI
Paper analysis often depends on understanding nuanced context beyond surface-level content
Combining research insights with practical coding involves tacit knowledge from education and experience

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for preliminary summarization and code scaffolding, but always reviews output
Does not rely on AI for critical research conclusions or mathematical proofs
Maintains a healthy skepticism toward AI-generated technical content

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive summarization and formatting tasks
Retains human control for creative, analytical, and decision-intensive work
Sees value in hybrid workflows where AI augments but does not replace human input

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for well-defined, low-risk tasks (e.g., code formatting)
Views automation as threatening if it encroaches on core analytical or research responsibilities
Desires clear override options whenever AI is applied to critical or creative tasks

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of domain expertise by offloading rote tasks
Sees coding skill as becoming more focused on problem-solving than syntax mastery
Views research and analytical skills as remaining essential despite AI adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Needs to develop prompt engineering skills for effective AI interactions
Gains responsibility for reviewing and validating AI-generated outputs
Exploring opportunities to integrate AI tools into existing workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Some concern about AI automating repetitive coding or summarization tasks
Does not feel immediate threat to core research, design, or analytical responsibilities
Views AI as a tool to augment rather than replace early-career technical roles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always manually reviews AI-generated summaries and code before use
Prefers workflows where human approval is required before finalizing AI outputs
Would not enable end-to-end AI automation without strong override controls

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: No formalized policy yet, but increasing openness to exploring AI tools
Team culture encourages experimentation with AI for productivity gains
Expects more structured guidelines for AI tool use to emerge in the near future

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Optimistic about AI’s potential to accelerate scientific discovery and innovation
Sees AI as transformative for fields involving large-scale data or repetitive analysis
Believes careful integration is needed to avoid over-reliance and maintain quality

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Concerned about potential loss of critical thinking if AI is used uncritically
Believes transparency and explainability are essential for ethical AI adoption
Supports strong human oversight for AI-driven decision-making in technical work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires reliable AI-powered summarization of academic papers highlighting novelty
Wants AI capable of implementing code from high-level functional descriptions
Seeks tools that can compare and contrast research methodologies automatically

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates more time spent on creative and analytical tasks as AI handles routine work
Expects to collaborate more closely with AI tools for literature review and coding
Foresees need to continuously update skills to effectively leverage AI advancements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement structured training sessions on effective and ethical AI tool usage
Develop clear guidelines for when and how AI outputs should be reviewed
Encourage experimentation with new AI features while maintaining human oversight
